problem: The Relation for the Conservation of Charge. The divergence of the current density is equal to the negative of the rate of change of charge density.
derivation: The divergence of the current density is equal to the negative of the rate of change of charge density.

problem: Coulomb’s Law. The magnitude of the force between two point charges is directly proportional to the product of the two charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
formulas: [In terms of the permittivity of free space] F = \fracq_1 q_24 pi epsilon_0 r^(2)
[Vector Form] F = - k_e \fracq_1 q_2| r |^(2) r_hat
[Vector form in terms of the permittivity of free space] F = - \fracq_1 q_24 pi epsilon_0 | r |^(2) r_hat

symbols.F: is the magnitude of the force experienced by each point charge (N)
symbols.k_e: is the Coulomb Constant, equal to $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}$ (N m^(2 C^-2))
symbols.q_1: is the electric charge of the first point charge (C)
symbols.q_2: is the electric charge of the second point charge (C)
symbols.r: is the distance between the point charges (m)

derivation: The magnitude of the force between two point charges is directly proportional to the product of the two charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

problem: The Definition of Electric Flux. The electric flux through a surface is equal to the integral of the scalar product of the electric field and the normal to the surface. In a uniform electric field, if a flat surface is perpendicular to the direction of the electric field, then the electric flux is equal to the field strength multiplied by the area of the surface. If the surface is parallel to the field, then the flux is zero.

symbols.S: is the surface being integrated over
symbols.d A: is an infinitesimal area on the surface $S$

derivation: The electric flux through a surface is equal to the integral of the scalar product of the electric field and the normal to the surface. In a uniform electric field, if a flat surface is perpendicular to the direction of the electric field, then the electric flux is equal to the field strength multiplied by the area of the surface. If the surface is parallel to the field, then the flux is zero.

problem: The Definition of the Magnetic Vector Potential. The magnetic vector potential is a vector potential such that its curl is the magnetic field.
derivation: The magnetic vector potential is a vector potential such that its curl is the magnetic field.

problem: The Definition of the Electric Field. The electric field is the electric force per unit charge exerted on a charged object.

symbols.F_e: is the electric force exerted on a charged object (N)
symbols.q: is the electric charge of the object (C)

derivation: The electric field is the electric force per unit charge exerted on a charged object.

problem: The Definition of the Poynting Vector. The Poynting Vector is proportional to the vector product of the electric and magnetic fields.

symbols.mu_0: is the permeability of free space

derivation: The Poynting Vector is proportional to the vector product of the electric and magnetic fields.

problem: The Definition of the Speed of Light. The speed of light can be defined in terms of the permittivity of free space and the permeability of free space.

symbols.c: is the speed of light in free space (m s^(-1))
symbols.epsilon_0: is the permittivity of free space
symbols.mu_0: is the permeability of free space

derivation: The speed of light can be defined in terms of the permittivity of free space and the permeability of free space.

problem: The Electric Field due to an Infinite Flat Surface of Charge. The magnitude of the electric field at a point due to an infinitely wide and long, flat surface of charge is constant everywhere, and depends only on the charge density per unit area of the surface. The direction of the electric field at that point is parallel to the normal to the surface.

symbols.sigma: is the charge density per unit area (C m^(-2))
symbols.\\varepsilon_0: is the permittivity of free space
symbols.n: is the normal to the surface

derivation: The magnitude of the electric field at a point due to an infinitely wide and long, flat surface of charge is constant everywhere, and depends only on the charge density per unit area of the surface. The direction of the electric field at that point is parallel to the normal to the surface.

problem: The Electric Field due to an Infinite Line of Charge. The magnitude of the electric field at a point due to an infinitely long line of charge is inversely proportional to the radial distance between the point and the line of charge, and the direction of the field at that point is always radial towards or away from the line of charge.

symbols.\\varepsilon_0: is the permittivity of free space
symbols.\\lambda: is the charge density per unit length (C m^(-1))
symbols.r: is the radial displacement of the point from the line of charge (m)

derivation: The magnitude of the electric field at a point due to an infinitely long line of charge is inversely proportional to the radial distance between the point and the line of charge, and the direction of the field at that point is always radial towards or away from the line of charge.

problem: The Formula for the Electric Field in terms of Potentials. The electric field is proportional to the sum of the rate of change of the magnetic vector potential and the gradient of the electric potential.
derivation: The electric field is proportional to the sum of the rate of change of the magnetic vector potential and the gradient of the electric potential.

problem: The Electric Field of a Point Charge. The magnitude of the electric field produced by a point charge is directly proportional to the value of the charge and inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the charge.
formulas: [In terms of the permittivity of free space] E = (q)/(4 pi epsilon_0) r^(2)
[Vector form in terms of the permittivity of free space] E = - (q)/(4 pi epsilon_0) | r |^(2) r_hat

symbols.E: is the magnitude of the electric field
symbols.k_e: is the Coulomb Constant, equal to $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}$
symbols.q: is the electric charge of the point charge
symbols.r: is the distance from the charge

derivation: The magnitude of the electric field produced by a point charge is directly proportional to the value of the charge and inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the charge.

problem: The Electric Potential Energy of Two Point Charges. The electric potential energy of two point charges is directly proportional to the product of the two charges, and inversely proportional to the distance between them.

symbols.k_e: is the Coulomb Constant, equal to $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}$
symbols.q_1: is the electric charge of the first point charge
symbols.q_2: is the electric charge of the second point charge
symbols.r: is the distance between the charges

derivation: The electric potential energy of two point charges is directly proportional to the product of the two charges, and inversely proportional to the distance between them.

problem: The Absolute Electric Potential of a Point Charge. The absolute electric potential at a distance $r$ from a point charge is directly proportional to the size of the charge, $q$, and inversely proportional to the distance $r$.

symbols.k_e: is the Coulomb Constant, equal to $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}$
symbols.q: is the electric charge of the point charge
symbols.r: is the distance from the charge

derivation: The absolute electric potential at a distance $r$ from a point charge is directly proportional to the size of the charge, $q$, and inversely proportional to the distance $r$.

problem: The Formula for the Flow Rate of Electromagnetic Momentum. The radiation pressure exerted by an electromagnetic wave is directly proportional to the magnitude of the Poynting Vector of the wave.

symbols.A: is the area of the surface that absorbs the wave
symbols.p: is the momentum of the wave
symbols.S: is the magnitude of the Poynting Vector
symbols.c: is the speed of light in free space
symbols.E: is the magnitude of the amplitude of the electric field
symbols.B: is the magnitude of the amplitude of the magnetic field
symbols.mu_0: is the permeability of free space

derivation: The radiation pressure exerted by an electromagnetic wave is directly proportional to the magnitude of the Poynting Vector of the wave.

problem: The Intensity of an Electromagnetic Wave in terms of the Poynting Vector. The intensity of an electromagnetic wave is the time-averaged value of the Poynting Vector.
formulas: [In terms of the electric field only] I = \fracE_\textmax^(2)2 mu_0 c

symbols.I: is the intensity of the electromagnetic wave
symbols.E_\\textmax: is the peak value of the electric field
symbols.B_\\textmax: is the peak value of the magnetic field
symbols.mu_0: is the permeability of free space

derivation: The intensity of an electromagnetic wave is the time-averaged value of the Poynting Vector.

problem: The Lorentz Force Equation. The Lorentz Force is the combination of the electric and magnetic force exerted on a point charge due to electric and magnetic fields.
formulas: F = q E + q v x B

symbols.q: is the charge of the particle (C)
symbols.v: is the velocity of the particle (m s^(-1))

derivation: The Lorentz Force is the combination of the electric and magnetic force exerted on a point charge due to electric and magnetic fields.

problem: The Formula for the Magnetic Field inside an Infinitely Long Solenoid. The strength of the magnetic field inside an infinitely long solenoid is proportional to the product of the current in the solenoid and the number of turns per unit length of the solenoid.
formulas: [In terms of the number of turns and the length of the solenoid] B = \fracmu_0 N IL

symbols.B: is the strength of the magnetic field
symbols.mu_0: is the permeability of free space
symbols.n: is the number of turns per unit length of the solenoid (m^(-1))
symbols.I: is the current in the solenoid (A)

derivation: The strength of the magnetic field inside an infinitely long solenoid is proportional to the product of the current in the solenoid and the number of turns per unit length of the solenoid.

problem: The Potential Energy of an Electric Dipole in an Electric Field. The potential energy of an electric dipole in an electric field is equal to the scalar product of the electric dipole moment and the electric field.
derivation: The potential energy of an electric dipole in an electric field is equal to the scalar product of the electric dipole moment and the electric field.

problem: The Formula for the Speed of Electromagnetic Waves in Matter. When an electromagnetic wave passes through a dielectric, the speed of the wave is less than the speed of light in a vacuum.

symbols.v: is the speed of light in the dielectric (m s^(-1))
symbols.epsilon: is the electric permittivity of the dielectric
symbols.mu: is the magnetic permeability of the dielectric

derivation: When an electromagnetic wave passes through a dielectric, the speed of the wave is less than the speed of light in a vacuum.

problem: The Torque on an Electric Dipole. The torque exerted on an electric dipole in the presence of an electric field is the vector product of the electric dipole moment and the electric field.
derivation: The torque exerted on an electric dipole in the presence of an electric field is the vector product of the electric dipole moment and the electric field.

problem: The Transformer Power Equation for an Ideal Transformer. For an ideal transformer, the product of the current in and potential difference across the primary coil is equal to the product of the current in and potential difference across the secondary coil.

symbols.I_P: is the current in the primary coil
symbols.V_P: is the potential difference across the primary coil
symbols.I_S: is the current in the secondary coil
symbols.V_S: is the potential difference across the secondary coil

derivation: For an ideal transformer, the product of the current in and potential difference across the primary coil is equal to the product of the current in and potential difference across the secondary coil.

problem: The Transformer Voltage Equation. The ratio of the voltage across the primary coil of a transformer to the voltage across the secondary coil of the transformer is equal to the ratio of the number of turns on the primary coil to the number of turns on the secondary coil.

symbols.V_P: is the voltage across the primary coil of the transformer
symbols.V_S: is the voltage across the secondary coil of the transformer
symbols.N_P: is the number of turns on the primary coil
symbols.N_S: is the number of turns on the secondary coil

derivation: The ratio of the voltage across the primary coil of a transformer to the voltage across the secondary coil of the transformer is equal to the ratio of the number of turns on the primary coil to the number of turns on the secondary coil.

problem: Physical constant: The Coulomb Constant. The Coulomb Constant is a constant of proportionality in the equations governing electrodynamics.
derivation: The Coulomb Constant is a constant of proportionality in the equations governing electrodynamics.

problem: Physical constant: Elementary Charge. The Elementary Charge is the charge carried by a single proton, and the magnitude of the charge carried by a single electron.
derivation: The Elementary Charge is the charge carried by a single proton, and the magnitude of the charge carried by a single electron.

problem: Physical constant: The Electron Rest Mass. The Electron Rest Mass is the mass of an electron as measured in the rest frame of an electron.
derivation: The Electron Rest Mass is the mass of an electron as measured in the rest frame of an electron.

problem: Physical constant: The Permeability of Free Space / The Vacuum Permeability. The permeability of free space is an ideal physical constant which is the value of magnetic permeability in a classical vacuum.

symbols.mu_0: The Permeability of Free Space / The Vacuum Permeability (N A^(-2))

derivation: The permeability of free space is an ideal physical constant which is the value of magnetic permeability in a classical vacuum.

problem: Physical constant: The Permittivity of Free Space / The Vacuum Permittivity. The permittivity of free space is an ideal physical constant which is the value of the absolute dielectric permittivity of a classical vacuum.

symbols.epsilon_0: The Permittivity of Free Space / The Vacuum Permittivity (F m^(-1))

derivation: The permittivity of free space is an ideal physical constant which is the value of the absolute dielectric permittivity of a classical vacuum.

problem: Physical constant: The Proton Rest Mass. The Proton Rest Mass is the mass of a proton as measured in the rest frame of a proton.
derivation: The Proton Rest Mass is the mass of a proton as measured in the rest frame of a proton.
